paper_title: Set-Based Transformer for Atmospheric Compensation in Standoff LWIR Hyperspectral Imaging

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Passive long-wave infrared (LWIR) hyperspectral imaging under a standoff geometry depends on atmospheric absorption and emission, as well as reflected radiance, thus making atmospheric compensation essential to get knowledge of a target of interest. Despite its importance, this compensation has been largely overlooked due to its practical and modeling difficulty. In this paper, we present a lightweight set-based deep learning framework that takes multiple radiance measurements, collected at different standoff ranges, as input and jointly estimates transmittance, atmospheric path radiance, and a shared downwelling spectrum. We analyze the learned representation with a sparse autoencoder and observe that several latent features do activate on geographically coherent subsets of the test data despite the absence of location supervision. Experiments on a MODTRAN generated standoff LWIR dataset demonstrate low spectral distortion across all estimated products. The dataset and code is publicly available at: https://factral.co/SAE-LWIR/

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 2026 IEEE. Personal use of this material is permitted. SET-BASED TRANSFORMER FOR ATMOSPHERIC COMPENSATION IN STANDOFF LWIR HYPERSPECTRAL IMAGING Fabian Perez , Nicolas Quintero , Jeferson Acevedo , Hoover Rueda-Chacon Department of Computer Science, Universidad Industrial de Santander Bucaramanga, 680002, Colombia {perez2258059,nicolas2220090,jeferson2221790}@correo.uis.edu.co, hfarueda@uis.edu.co 6 2 0 2 ] . [ 1 4 2 3 8 0 . 6 0 6 2 : r AbstractPassive long-wave infrared (LWIR) hyperspectral imaging under standoff geometry depends on atmospheric absorption and emission, as well as reflected radiance, thus making atmospheric compensation essential to get knowledge of target of interest. Despite its importance, this compensation has been largely overlooked due to its practical and modeling difficulty. In this paper, we present lightweight set-based deep learning framework that takes multiple radiance measurements, collected at different standoff ranges, as input and jointly estimates transmittance, atmospheric path radiance, and shared downwelling spectrum. We analyze the learned representation with sparse autoencoder and observe that several latent features do activate on geographically coherent subsets of the test data despite the absence of location supervision. Experiments on MODTRAN generated standoff LWIR dataset demonstrate low spectral distortion across all estimated products. The dataset and code is publicly available at: https://factral.co/SAE-LWIR/ Index TermsLWIR, atmospheric compensation, hyperspectral imaging, sparse autoencoder. I. INTRODUCTION Unlike the visible to shortwave infrared (VISSWIR) range, which is dominated by reflected solar radiation (0.42.5 µm) [1], the long-wave infrared (LWIR) senses thermally emitted radiation within the 814 µm atmospheric window [2]. This enables sensing capabilities independent of solar illumination, thus making LWIR suitable for daynight operation. As result, LWIR has been widely used in security and surveillance [3, 4], autonomous driving and infrastructure monitoring [5, 6], wildfire detection [7], environmental remote sensing [8], and hyperspectral target detection [9]. LWIR measurements acquired under standoff sensing configuration are governed by the radiative transfer equation (RTE) and are affected by atmospheric attenuation through the path transmittance τ , atmospheric absorption and self-emission La (upwelling), as well as by surface reflection Lref driven by downwelling Ld. The forward standoff model, following the RTE in [2, 10], is given by (cid:104) L(λ; rn; ) = τ (λ; rn) ε B(λ; ) (cid:124) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) Lobj + ρ Ld(λ) (cid:124) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) Lref (cid:105) + La(λ; rn). (1) where L(λ; rn; ) denotes the at-sensor radiance at wavelength λ for the n-th standoff range (distance) rn and target temperature , ε is the object emmisivity and ρ is the Fig. 1. Standoff LWIR imaging configuration. The atmosphere is discretized into 126 layers. Downwelling irradiance from the sky Ld illuminates the target. The at-sensor radiance at the hyperspectral LWIR camera is shown as the sum of (i) target thermal emission Lobj , (ii) reflected downwelling irradiance Lref , and (iii) atmospheric path emission along the line of sight La surface albedo. Unlike airborne and satellite remote sensing, standoff acquisition operates at shorter ranges where the same scene may be observed across multiple standoff distances, making atmospheric effects range-dependent as depicted in Fig. 1. Hence, accurate downstream tasks on the target require atmospheric compensation (AC), which demands the estimation of transmittance, upwelling, and downwelling (TUD). Building on this, the AC literature has largely focused on airbone and satellite rather than standoff sensing. In near-nadir viewing geometry, the upwelling radiance and transmittance can be treated as approximately the same for all pixels [11], which simplifies estimation. State-of-the-art works like [12] proposed neural network to estimate atmospheric components jointly with land-surface temperature, but the method is tailored to satellite nadir observations. In [13], variational autoencoder (VAE) learning framework is proposed, again restricted to the nadir regime. Oblique remote configurations, where upwelling and transmittance vary across the field of view have been explored in [14], but assume known range or rely on MODTRAN [15] (MODerate resolution atmospheric TRANsmission) look-up tables for AC estimation. Similarly, [16] proposes convolutional neural network (CNN) to estimate atmospheric products (TUD estimation) but evaluates only remote configurations. In standoff LWIR, near-horizontal and range-diverse paths make the radiative terms strongly geometry-dependent, and the problem remains comparatively underexplored. In [17], Fig. 2. Proposed Framework. The input consists of radiance measurements selected from the globally generated dataset by sampling single location (pink dots on the globe) and extracting its observations at different standoff ranges; each sample has = 256 spectral bands beetween 8µm and 13µm, stacked as RN B. Set-Transformer encoder with two ISAB modules encode the set of measurements into latent representation. The latent features are then routed to three task-specific decoders: an FFN with sigmoid activation predicts atmospheric transmittance ˆYτ RN B; an FFN predicts atmospheric path radiance ˆYa RN B; PMA module followed by an MLP aggregates across the set to estimate shared downwelling spectrum ˆyd RB. diffusion-based framework models long-range absorption for VIS/NIR/SWIR but does not address LWIR. In [18], CNN-based approach is proposed but neglects the atmospheric path-radiance contribution. In [19], absorption is leveraged to estimate depth, while omitting the reflected downwelling term in the RTE. Collectively, these limitations highlight the need for AC methods that explicitly handle LWIR standoff imaging. In this work, we propose lightweight set-based deep learning framework for atmospheric compensation in passive standoff LWIR imaging. Here, set-based refers to jointly processing multiple radiance measurements acquired at different standoff ranges, without assuming any ordering [20]. By jointly exploiting multiple radiance measurements acquired at different standoff ranges, the method estimates the following AC products: atmospheric transmittance (τ ), upwelling radiance (La), and shared downwelling spectrum (Ld). The main contributions are listed as follows: set-based framework for joint TUD estimation in passive standoff LWIR imaging, coupled with sparse autoencoder to enable latent-space interpretability. The first publicly available MODTRAN-generated dataset for atmospheric compensation in standoff LWIR imaging. II. PROPOSED METHOD We propose set-based framework to estimate AC products from standoff hyperspectral LWIR observations as illustrated in Fig. 2. Each sample input consist of measurements acquired at different standoff ranges, each represented as spectrum over spectral bands. We first describe the neural network that predicts the AC products, and then introduce sparse autoencoder that exposes structure in the learned latent representations. A. Set-based Neural Network Let the n-th standoff measurement, acquired at range rn, be radiance spectrum xn RB, and let the input set be written as RN whose rows are the elements {xn}N n=1 collected at distinct standoff ranges = {rn}N n=1. Note that multiple ranges provide additional constraints that mitigate the ill-posed problem of estimating the (AC) products, since τ and La vary with rn. The network predicts three outputs: range-wise transmittance ˆYτ RN B, range-wise upwelling ˆYa RN B, and shared downwelling spectrum ˆyd RB. We denote the three task-specific branches as ˆYτ = GT (X), ˆYa = GU (X), ˆyd = Fd(X). (2) Symmetry requirements. Because the measurements form set, their ordering is arbitrary. Let {0, 1}N be permutation matrix and denote the row-permuted input by X. Accordingly, ˆYτ and ˆYa are predicted per standoff range, so each output must stay aligned with the same input; thus, each branch must be permutation equivariant, that is, GT (P X) = GT (X), GU (P X) = GU (X). (3) In contrast, downwelling is shared across the standoff ranges, for fixed atmosphere, and therefore must be permutation invariant, which can be denoted as Fd(P X) = Fd(X). (4) Set encoder. To satisfy the requirements described above, we adopt Set-Transformer encoder [21]. Specifically, we use Fig. 3. Sparse Autoencoder pipeline. Encoder tokens Z2 are the input to the SAE that reconstructs token activations by minimizing LSAE. ISAB (Induced Set Attention Block) which is built from Multihead Attention Block (MAB) [22]. Using inducing points Rmd, ISAB introduces compact set of learnable latent anchors that attend to the input set to form global summary S, and then lets each element in attend to this summary [21]. ISAB can be expressed as: ISABm(X) = MAB(X, S) RN d, where = MAB(I, X) Rmd. (5) (6) We use two stacked ISAB layers to increase the depth of the set encoder so as to capture higher-order interactions Z2 = ISAB2 (cid:0)ISAB1(X)(cid:1). (7) ISAB is permutation equivariant by construction, so permuting the input permutes the latent tokens in the same way [21]. Equivariant transmittance and upwelling decoders. The transmittance and upwelling branches apply the same feedforward network (FFN) independently to each latent token. Concisely, we use two token-wise FFNs, FFNϕ and FFNψ, each mapping Rd RB and applied to the encoder output Z2: ˆYτ = σ(cid:0)FFNϕ(Z2)(cid:1), ˆYa = FFNψ(Z2), (8) where σ() is an element-wise sigmoid that enforces ˆYτ (0, 1), and FFNϕ, FFNψ to share the same structure but to not share weights across tasks. For the n-th token zn, we define the token-wise FFN output by (cid:2)FFN(Z2)(cid:3) zn = [Z2]n. := FFN(zn), (9) The FFN computes h(1) (cid:16) = LN1 (cid:16) = LN2 h(2) GELU(W1zn + b1) (cid:17) GELU(W2h(1) + b2) , (cid:17) (10) , where LN denotes layer normalization [23] and GELU refer to the activation function [24], and outputs + b3, on RB. on = W3h(2) (11) n=1 yields FFN(Z2) RN B. Applying the Stacking {on}N FFN to every token makes the output permutation-equivariant. Invariant downwelling decoder. We assume downwelling is common to all the measurements in set, since they are acquired under the same atmosphere; therefore, we aggregate Z2 into single vector using Pooling by Multihead Attention (PMA) [21]. PMA introduces learned seed vectors Rkd and pools the set through attention as implemented by = PMA(Z2) := MAB(S, Z2) Rkd. We use k=1 so that the pooled embedding is Rd. We then post-process this pooled representation with linear projection to obtain the B-band downwelling estimate: ˆyd = MLP(cid:0)v(cid:1), where MLP(v) := Wd + bd, ˆd RB, (13) (14) (12) Training objective. We train the network by minimizing the mean squared error (MSE) between the predicted atmospheric products ( ˆYτ , ˆYa, ˆyd) and their corresponding ground-truth targets (Yτ , Ya, yd). We define the per-sample losses as (cid:13) ˆYτ Yτ (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) 2 (cid:13) (cid:13) 2 , Lτ = La = Ld = ˆyd yd2 2 , and optimize the total objective L, (cid:13) ˆYa Ya (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) 2 (cid:13) (cid:13) 2 , B. Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) = Lτ + La + Ld. (15) (16) To better understand what the trained model encodes and whether its internal features align with physically meaningful factors, we analyze the frozen trained set encoder using sparse autoencoder (SAE) [25]. Concretely, given an input set X, the encoder outputs Z2 RN d, and we treat each token zn = [Z2]n Rd as an activation vector to be reconstructed with sparse, overcomplete representation as depicted in Fig. 3. Let Rd denote token activation, i.e., = zn. The SAE encoder produces sparse features RM using TopK gating [25]: := TopK(cid:0)Wencx + benc (cid:1), Wenc RM d, (17) where TopK() retains only the largest-magnitude entries and sets the remaining activations to zero. The decoder then reconstructs the token activation with linear dictionary: ˆx = aWdec + bdec, Wdec RM d, bdec Rd. (18) We train the SAE using the per-token ℓ2 reconstruction loss: LSAE(x) = ˆx2 2 . (19) III. DATASET GENERATION To the best of our knowledge, there is currently no publicly available dataset tailored to AC in standoff LWIR imaging. To address this gap, we generate large-scale simulated dataset using the clear-sky atmospheric profile database (CSP) [26]. This database is derived from the European Centre for MediumRange Weather Forecast Reanalysis V5 (ERA5) and contains 82,828 profiles with key atmospheric state variables, including latitude, longitude, pressure, temperature, specific humidity and ozone concentration at 136 layers ln. We further filter these profiles by discarding cases with cloud coverage exceeding 10% or relative humidity above 90%, and by removing subset of ocean-surface profiles, thus yielding 36,547 clearsky profiles. Radiance at-sensor was simulated using seven target temperatures ranging from 280K to 310K inclusive, assuming fixed gray-body like emissivity ε value of 0.95. TABLE TEST-SET PERFORMANCE FOR DIFFERENT SET SIZES . Target 7 Transmittance Upwelling Downwelling Transmittance Upwelling Downwelling SAM 0.0057 0.1244 0.1937 0.0025 0.0330 0.0409 NRMSE 0.0554 0.0564 0.1740 0.0093 0.0093 0.0193 MODTRAN5 [15], accessed through an internally developed Python tool, was used to calculate atmospheric products and the at-sensor radiance following Eq. 1. Specifically, it computes band-integrated at-sensor radiance convolving with the instrumental spectral response function (ISRF), and we report radiance in microflicks (µW sr1 cm2 µm1). Gas concentrations were specified independently for each of the first 126 layers ℓ126 (maximum number in MODTRAN5) using the filtered database. Downwelling radiance Ld was calculated at fixed viewing angle of 45 from the target as an approximated representation of the hemispherical sky radiance incident on the target. We define = 7 and = {30, 90, 150, 210, 270, 330, 390} m. The atmospheric path radiance La (upwelling) and transmittance τ were computed along the line of sight for each rn, as illustrated in Fig. 1. The resulting standoff MODTRAN-generated dataset has 36,547 profiles, 7 ranges, and 7 temperatures, yielding total of 255,829 samples. The dataset was randomly split into 70% for training, 10% for validation, and 20% for testing. IV. RESULTS AND ANALYSIS We evaluate on the test split of the generated standoff LWIR dataset. We use = 1 with rn = 270m and = 7 standoff measurements per sample, and an encoder embedding size of = 256. The spectral dimension is set to = 256, consistent with the bands of real standoff sensor, the DARPA LWIR hyperspectral sensor [19, 27] over the 813 µm window; we model each channel spectral response as Gaussian with FWHM = 40 nm. Training is performed for 510k iterations with batch size 512 and learning rate 1 103, using AdamW with weight decay 0.01. All experiments are run on single NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell GPU; training requires only 10% of the available VRAM, indicating that the proposed architecture is lightweight and scalable. Tab. reports testset performance for the three estimated AC products. We report Spectral Angle Mapper (SAM) and normalized RMSE (NRMSE). As expected, performance improves substantially Fig. 4. Qualitative results on one test sample predicted (dashed) and groundtruth (solid) are shown for transmittance Yτ , atmospheric path radiance Ya, and downwelling radiance yd across the LWIR window. The left column shows the full spectral range, while the right column provides zoomed view. Fig. 5. Top-activating locations for two SAE features. Each point denotes test sample among the highest activations. from = 1 to = 7, since single-measurement estimation is inherently ill-posed while range-diverse measurements better constrain the solution. Overall, the model achieves low spectral distortion across all AC products, with particularly strong agreement for transmittance. Fig. 4 shows qualitative predictions for one randomly selected test sample, including the estimated ˆYτ , ˆYa, and ˆyd compared against their corresponding groundtruths. The plotted spectra highlight that the network preserves fine-grained spectral structure. SAE analysis. To better understand the latent space learned by the encoder, we train SAE on the frozen activations Z2 RN d. We use sparse feature dimension of = 3072 (i.e., 12d) to decouple latent factors, and we optimize the SAE for 20k iterations using AdamW. The TopK gating parameter is set to = 16, selected via cross-validation. After training, we perform top-activating analysis [28] to identify the samples that most strongly activate each sparse feature. Interestingly, for multiple features, the highest-activation examples consistently correspond to radiance sets originating from highly similar geographic regions, despite the fact the network is never provided with explicit location. This emergent behavior suggests that the model organizes the features by clustering atmospheres with similar physical conditions, Fig. 5 plots the top-activating examples for two sparse features; note how the activations form clear location-related clusters. that V. CONCLUSIONS We presented set-based deep learning framework for standoff LWIR atmospheric compensation that estimates transmittance, atmospheric path radiance, and shared downwelling radiance from range-diverse measurements. We showed that SAE provides powerful interpretability tool for probing physically grounded representations. Future work will model angle-dependent downwelling, incorporate spectrally varying emissivity, and study sensitivity to the number of measurements and the selected standoff ranges, while exploring alternative SAE variants and model capacities. VI.
